g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on parallel memory architectures - the foundation of high-performance computing.
In parallel computing, memory architecture determines how processors access data - the core design choice shaping all software.
g.text: The fundamental distinction in parallel computing is how multiple processors access memory.
This choice determines your programming model, communication strategy, and scalability.

g.data-narration: The fundamental difference is address space: shared memory has ONE global address space, distributed has MANY private spaces.
In shared-memory systems, all CPUs connect to a common memory bank through an interconnect, accessing the same address space.
Shared-memory comes in two flavors: UMA offers equal access time everywhere, while NUMA varies access based on memory location.
g.text: • Equal access time to all memory
• Access time depends on memory location

g.data-narration: OpenMP programs use pragmas to create parallel threads that share data - simple but requires synchronization primitives.
Shared memory offers easier programming with global address space, dynamic load balancing, and no explicit data partitioning.

g.text: Ease of programming - global address space is easier to reason about
Dynamic load balancing - can allocate work dynamically

g.data-narration: The key challenges are scalability limits from bus bandwidth, cache coherence overhead, and complex synchronization leading to deadlocks.
Distributed-memory systems have independent nodes, each with private memory, communicating via high-speed network interconnects.
MPI uses explicit message-passing - programmers send and receive data between processes, managing all communication themselves.
g.text: • Each process has private address space

g.data-narration: Distributed memory scales to thousands of cores, has no cache coherence overhead, and uses cost-effective commodity hardware.
Distributed memory programming is complex with explicit communication, network latency overhead, and requires careful load balancing.

g.text: Complex programming - must manage data decomposition and all communication
Communication overhead - network latency/bandwidth can bottleneck
Load balancing - must statically balance work to avoid idle processors

g.data-narration: This table summarizes key differences: address space, data sharing, scalability, programming model, and typical use cases.
Modern HPC uses hybrid systems: MPI between nodes for distributed communication, OpenMP within nodes for shared-memory parallelism.
g.text: Most modern supercomputers combine both approaches:

g.data-narration: Remember: shared memory has ONE address space (OpenMP), distributed has MANY (MPI). The choice depends on your scalability needs.

g.text: Fundamental: Address space - ONE vs MANY
